speaker: patient
text: Hi doctor, I’ve been feeling nauseous since yesterday morning.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in. Let me check your vitals right away — your heart rate is elevated at 114 beats per minute.

speaker: doctor
text: Your blood glucose is 8.8 mmol/L — that’s above target range for fasting, especially given your symptoms.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure is 97 mmHg — a bit high, which we’ll monitor closely.

speaker: doctor
text: I see you’re currently on insulin glargine 16 units nightly. How consistent have you been with dosing and timing?

speaker: doctor
text: Your temperature is 37.6 °C — mildly elevated but not yet febrile.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve taken the insulin every night as prescribed, but I skipped dinner twice this week because of the nausea.

speaker: doctor
text: That could explain the hyperglycemia despite insulin — missing meals can disrupt glucose balance and increase stress hormones.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your history, labs, and exam, your primary diagnosis is Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, currently decompensated.

speaker: patient
text: Does that mean my diabetes is getting worse? I’ve also had some dizziness when standing up.

speaker: doctor
text: Dizziness could relate to orthostatic changes or volume status — let’s reassess your BP and pulse after you sit up.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, I feel a little lightheaded now just sitting here.

speaker: doctor
text: Let me recheck your heart rate — it’s now 85 bpm, much more stable than earlier.

speaker: doctor
text: Actually, when I checked again just now, your heart rate is 85 bpm — quite a drop from the 114 we saw at the start.

speaker: doctor
text: And your temperature has risen to 38.9 °C — that suggests an acute infectious process may be contributing to your nausea and metabolic instability.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your elevated diastolic pressure and new fever, I’m going to add amlodipine 5 mg daily to help control vascular tone while we address the underlying trigger.

speaker: patient
text: Amlodipine? I’ve never taken that before — will it interact with my insulin?

speaker: doctor
text: No significant interaction — it works on calcium channels in blood vessels, not glucose metabolism. We’ll watch for ankle swelling or flushing.

speaker: patient
text: Got it. By the way, I checked my blood sugar again this morning — same number as before: 8.8 mmol/L.

speaker: doctor
text: Yes, that reading matches the one from earlier today — persistent hyperglycemia despite basal insulin, which reinforces the need to investigate for infection or other stressors.
